text:
MAGNETIC RESONANCE IMAGING - BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

EXAMINATION: MRI Brain with and without contrast (gadolinium)
INDICATION: 63-year-old female with newly diagnosed right renal cell carcinoma (clear cell type, Fuhrman grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation), T3b with macroscopic renal vein thrombus, N1 retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with M1 disease. MRI brain performed for metastatic staging workup prior to surgical and systemic therapy planning.
COMPARISON: No prior brain MRI available for comparison.
TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain performed on a 3.0 Tesla MR unit. Sequences obtained: T1-weighted sagittal localizer, axial DWI (b=0 and b=1000), axial FLAIR, axial T2-weighted, axial GRE/susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), axial T1 pre- and post-gadolinium (Gadavist 0.1 mmol/kg IV), coronal T1 post-contrast, 3D MPRAGE post-contrast. No significant motion degradation. Contrast agent administered without complication.

---

FINDINGS:

SUPRATENTORIAL STRUCTURES:
The cerebral hemispheres demonstrate normal gray-white matter differentiation bilaterally. No abnormal T1 or T2/FLAIR signal abnormality is identified within the cerebral cortex, white matter, or deep gray matter nuclei. No areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute or subacute infarction. No cortical or subcortical enhancing lesions are identified on the post-gadolinium sequences. No leptomeningeal enhancement. The periventricular and subcortical white matter demonstrates two to three punctate T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the bilateral frontal lobes, measuring up to 3 mm each, without associated mass effect, restricted diffusion, or enhancement -- these are most consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic changes of the degree expected for the patient's age and comorbidities (hypertension, diabetes mellitus). No confluent white matter signal abnormality.

BASOGANGLIA AND THALAMI:
The basal ganglia, thalami, and internal capsules appear symmetric and within normal limits bilaterally. No signal abnormality. No enhancing lesions.

BRAINSTEM AND CEREBELLUM:
The brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla) is unremarkable in signal and morphology. No intrinsic signal abnormality. No enhancing lesion. The cerebellar hemispheres and vermis appear symmetric and without signal abnormality. No cerebellar enhancing lesions identified.

VENTRICLES AND SULCI:
The lateral, third, and fourth ventricles are normal in size and configuration without evidence of obstructive hydrocephalus. The cerebral sulci and Sylvian fissures are appropriate for the patient's stated age. No midline shift. No evidence of mass effect or herniation.

SELLA / PARASELLAR REGION:
The pituitary gland is of normal size and morphology. No sellar or parasellar mass. The optic chiasm appears normal.

INTRACRANIAL VASCULATURE (FLOW VOIDS):
Major intracranial vascular flow voids are preserved. No focal areas of susceptibility artifact to suggest cavernous malformation, hemorrhagic metastasis, or microhemorrhage on SWI sequences beyond expected background.

DURAL VENOUS SINUSES:
The major dural venous sinuses appear patent based on standard sequences. No evidence of dural-based metastatic enhancement or abnormal dural thickening.

LEPTOMENINGEAL ASSESSMENT:
No abnormal leptomeningeal or pachymeningeal enhancement identified. No sulcal FLAIR signal abnormality to suggest leptomeningeal carcinomatosis.

BONE AND SOFT TISSUES:
The calvarium and skull base demonstrate no lytic or blastic osseous lesions. The visualized orbits, mastoid air cells, and paranasal sinuses are unremarkable. No scalp soft tissue mass.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. NO EVIDENCE OF INTRACRANIAL METASTATIC DISEASE. No parenchymal enhancing lesions, leptomeningeal enhancement, or dural-based metastases are identified.

2. No acute intracranial abnormality. No hemorrhage, infarction, mass effect, or midline shift.

3. A small number (2-3) of punctate T2/FLAIR white matter hyperintensities in the bilateral frontal lobes, most consistent with chronic microvascular ischemic changes in the setting of known hypertension and diabetes mellitus. These are of no acute clinical significance and are not suggestive of metastatic disease.

4. No osseous calvaria or skull base metastases identified on brain MRI.

In summary, this MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast demonstrates no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease at the time of initial staging for newly diagnosed stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma.
